Created data/schemas/attack-tree.yaml — AND/OR attack tree schema for Call 2 output. Schema defines: recursive node type with id, label, gate (AND/OR/LEAF), zone (1-5), threat_id, technique_id, maestro_layer, control_point, structural_exposure (4 enum values), evidence_level (5 enum values). Includes validation rules (LEAF has no children, AND/OR >= 2 children, max depth 5, dotted ID paths). Complete example based on T2-S1 Parameter Pollution with 13 nodes across 3 levels, 3 zones, 3 ATLAS techniques. Designed for direct Pydantic translation and LLM structured output.

Designed scenario envelope schema at data/schemas/scenario-envelope.yaml. Defines the top-level document structure wrapping all 4 layers (narrative, attack tree, behavior spec, faceting metadata) plus identity, priority signals, and generation metadata. Includes sub-schemas for NarrativeStep and CallMetadata. Attack tree and behavior spec are opaque sub-document slots referencing their own schemas (scenario-forge-13k, scenario-forge-1ty). Contains a complete example using T2-S1 Parameter Pollution with realistic faceting metadata, multi-level success criteria, and priority signals. Schema uses type/enum/constraint annotations ready for Pydantic translation.

Delivered behavior-spec-variants.yaml with three parts: (1) Pure Gherkin specs for T2-S2, T1-S4, T12-S4, T9-S6; (2) Gherkin-Plus variants with attack_shape phases, multi-level success_criteria with confidence/depends_on, metadata linkage, and preconditions; (3) Comparison recommending Gherkin-Plus — better for attack shapes, multi-level criteria, LLM structured output, and multi-agent/non-conversational scenarios.

Wrote capability profile schema to data/schemas/capability-profile.yaml. Schema defines: (1) Stage 1 required fields — zones_active, has_persistent_memory, multi_agent, hitl, entry_points, confidence — with types, valid values, constraints, and descriptions. (2) Stage 2 optional fields — tool_types, data_flows, trust_boundaries, memory_mechanisms, external_integrations — with full item schemas. (3) Depth setting enum (minimal/moderate/thorough) documenting which Stage 2 fields each level populates. (4) Threat scope mapping documenting which capability flags gate which threats: always-in-scope (T6/T7/T8/T15), memory-gated (T1/T5), tool-execution-gated (T2/T3/T4/T9/T11/T17), HITL-gated (T10), multi-agent-gated (T12/T13/T14/T16), with sub-scenario gating. (5) Two complete examples: simple FAQ chatbot (minimal, narrow scope: 4 threats) and multi-agent customer service platform (moderate, full scope: all 17 threats).

QA review complete. 1 medium finding, 1 low finding. MEDIUM: Example tree node n1.2.1 uses technique_id AML.T0051.000 which is not a valid ATLAS sub-technique (sub-techniques start from .001, not .000). Fix: change to AML.T0051 (base technique) or AML.T0051.001 if a specific sub-technique is intended. LOW: The schema regex pattern ^AML\.T\d{4}(\.\d{3})?$ accepts .000 sub-techniques which do not exist in the ATLAS taxonomy. Fix: tighten to ^AML\.T\d{4}(\.\d{3})?$ with an additional validation rule excluding .000, or document this is intentionally permissive. All other checklist items pass: all 7 per-node metadata fields present, AND/OR/LEAF gate semantics correct, validation rules cover key edge cases (empty children, min 2 children, depth limit), structural_exposure enums match §7.3, evidence_level enums match §9.1, example tree is realistic for T2-S1 Parameter Pollution with correct zone assignments, schema is compact for LLM structured output, MAESTRO layers 1-7 are correct per Schneider.

QA complete. Schema is well-aligned with the design doc. Found 2 issues:

MEDIUM: Example owasp_llm_ids inconsistent with cross-taxonomy-mappings. Example uses ["LLM06", "LLM01"] for a T2 scenario, but T2 maps to LLM06/LLM03/LLM08 per the mappings file. LLM01 belongs to T6/T11, not T2. Fix: change to ["LLM06", "LLM03"] or ["LLM06"].

LOW: scenario_id format (<threat_id>-<sub_scenario_seed>-<hash>) omits risk_id from the string, though the design doc §9.3 defines stable identity as the (risk_id, threat_id, sub_scenario_seed) tuple. Two different risk cards seeding the same threat+scenario would produce colliding ID prefixes. Fix: either embed risk_id in the ID format or document that the hash disambiguates cross-risk-card collisions.

Everything else checks out: all §6.3 faceting fields present, priority signals match §7.1-7.2 exactly (including composite 0-1), narrative correctly separates deterministic pre-filled fields from LLM prose, attack_tree and behavior_spec are properly opaque slots, generation metadata captures model/tokens/timing/notes, and ID format conventions match the taxonomy files.

QA review of capability-profile.yaml against design doc and taxonomies. 3 findings:

FINDING 1 (HIGH): T16 mis-gated. Schema places T16 (Insecure Inter-Agent Protocol Abuse) under multi_agent_gated, but OWASP decision tree puts T16 at Step 3 (tool/execution/supply chain threats, decision_tree_step: 3). T16 is explicitly categorized as "Tool, execution and supply chain-based threats" in the source taxonomy. Fix: move T16 from multi_agent_gated to tool_execution_gated. This changes scope for single-agent systems that use MCP tool servers (T16 would now apply if zone 3 is active, even without multi-agent).

FINDING 2 (MEDIUM): T15 placement diverges from source. Schema makes T15 (Human Manipulation) always-in-scope, but OWASP decision tree gates it at Step 5 alongside T10. The rationale is defensible (any user-facing AI can manipulate humans), but this is a design decision diverging from the literal taxonomy. Fix: document this as an explicit design decision in the schema comments, not just in the rationale text.

FINDING 3 (MEDIUM): Example 2 incorrectly excludes T1-S4. The example has a shared key_value_store (scope: shared) but claims T1-S4 is excluded because "memory is per-user, not shared." Either include T1-S4 in the example (shared memory exists), or refine the T1-S4 gating rule to require writable shared memory (the shared store is read-only in the example).

PASSED items: Stage 1 fields match §4.2 exactly (6/6 fields). Stage 2 fields correctly cover depth table. Depth settings match §4.2 table verbatim. Schneider zone numbering correct (1-5). Agentic-only threats (T7,T8,T9,T10,T14,T15,T16) correctly reflected. Sub-scenario gating for T1-S4, T2-S4, T2-S5 is correct against source. Both examples are realistic. Schema supports reviewable/editable artifact requirement from §4.1.

Added risk_description to RiskCardRef and populated it in _make_risk_card_ref()

INVESTIGATION FINDINGS: Null values in attack tree output

ROOT CAUSE: This is a serialization issue, not an LLM or parsing issue. The nulls are caused by Pydantic model_dump() serializing Optional fields with default=None as explicit null values in the YAML output, combined with the fact that the LLM legitimately does not populate every optional field on every node.

BREAKDOWN BY CATEGORY:

1. EXPECTED NULLS (Optional fields the LLM reasonably leaves empty):
   - description (77/83 nodes null) — prompt says "optional longer description"
   - threat_id (61/83 null) — only relevant on some nodes
   - technique_id (79/83 null) — most nodes lack ATLAS technique IDs
   - maestro_layer (63/83 null) — only relevant on some nodes
   - control_point (45/83 null) — not every step has a defensive control
   - structural_exposure (54/83 null) — most nodes lack structural exposure

2. STRUCTURAL NULLS (always null on LEAF nodes):
   - children (51 nulls) — LEAF nodes have no children; serialized as null instead of omitted

3. TOP-LEVEL NULLS (non-tree fields):
   - notes (7/7 files) — GenerationMetadata.notes: Optional[list[str]] = None; generate.py passes empty list which becomes None
   - atlas_technique_ids (7/7 files) — TaxonomyChain.atlas_technique_ids: Optional[list[str]] = None; some seeds lack ATLAS IDs

ANALYSIS:
- The LLM output is correct — these fields ARE optional in the model and the prompt says "Add optional fields where appropriate"
- The YAML parsing in _parse_attack_tree_yaml() is working correctly
- The Pydantic model definitions are correct in making these fields Optional
- The only issue is COSMETIC: model_dump(mode="json") includes None values, which yaml.dump serializes as the literal text "null"

FIX RECOMMENDATION:
In generate.py line 790, change:
  data = envelope.model_dump(mode="json")
to:
  data = envelope.model_dump(mode="json", exclude_none=True)

This single change will:
- Omit all null fields from the YAML output
- Keep populated fields intact
- Reduce output file size significantly (each file has 40-60 null lines)
- Make the YAML much cleaner and easier to read

SECONDARY CONSIDERATION:
If the team prefers explicit null markers for some fields (to distinguish "not populated" from "field doesn't exist"), an alternative is to keep exclude_none=False but change specific model defaults:
- children: change default from None to [] (empty list) — but this breaks the LEAF validation
- evidence_level already defaults to "assumed" — no null issue
- notes: change empty list handling in _assemble_envelope to pass [] instead of None

The exclude_none=True approach is strongly recommended as the simplest, most comprehensive fix.
